title: Linking & Cohesion — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: Discourse markers + reference + substitution + paragraph flow — Band 7's own scoring criterion
intro: Coherence & Cohesion is one of the four IELTS Writing scoring criteria — 25% of your mark. Band 7 says 'uses cohesion well'; Band 8 says 'cohesion attracts no attention'. This means: don't open every paragraph with 'Firstly / Secondly / In conclusion'. The Band 7+ writer uses VARIED linkers, REFERENCE devices (this, such, the former), and SUBSTITUTION (one, do so, so) to glue ideas together invisibly.

sections.title: 1. Adding Ideas — Beyond 'Also'
sections.summary: If you only know 'and / also / moreover', you sound Band 6. Each linker has a register and rhythm.

items: furthermore (similar to moreover, slightly more formal)
in addition to + noun/-ing (In addition to causing pollution, cars contribute to noise)
besides + noun/-ing (Besides being expensive, the policy is impractical)
not to mention + noun (The plan is impractical, not to mention costly)

examples.plural: Not only does pollution harm health, but it also damages the economy.
The proposal is impractical. What is more, it is prohibitively expensive.
Wages are stagnant. On top of that, the cost of living continues to rise.

items: Firstly… Secondly… Thirdly… Finally… (basic — don't repeat across all paragraphs)
For one thing… For another thing…
On the one hand… On the other hand… (NOT 'in the other hand')

sections.tip: In a 4-paragraph essay, never use 'Firstly… Secondly… Thirdly… Finally…' as the openers. Mix: paragraph 1 opens with 'One key reason', paragraph 2 with 'A further consideration', paragraph 3 with 'Equally important'. Variety = Band 7+ cohesion.

sections.title: 2. Contrast & Concession — Beyond 'However'
sections.summary: Contrast linkers carry different shades of opposition. Mix at least 3–4 different ones per essay.

examples.plural: I wanted to go, but I was busy.
I wanted to go. However, I was busy.

examples.singular: nevertheless / nonetheless (in spite of what was said)
examples.plural: The plan is risky. Nevertheless, it deserves consideration.
Some people prefer city life, whereas others find it stressful.
While technology brings convenience, it also creates new dependencies.

rules.pattern: ALTHOUGH / EVEN THOUGH / THOUGH (+ clause) — concession

examples.plural: Although it was raining, we went out.
Even though the policy is unpopular, it is necessary.
It's expensive, though.

rules.pattern: DESPITE / IN SPITE OF (+ noun / -ing) — concession

examples.plural: Despite the rain, we went out.
In spite of the criticism, the policy was implemented.
Despite being tired, she finished the report.
Despite the fact that costs have risen, demand remains high.

rules.note: ❌ Critical error: 'Despite he was tired' / 'In spite of she was busy'. After 'despite/in spite of', use NOUN or -ING — not a clause. To use a clause, say 'despite the fact that' or switch to 'although'.

items: albeit (= although; followed by adjective/phrase, not a full clause): 'The policy is effective, albeit expensive.'
notwithstanding (formal): 'Notwithstanding these concerns, the project will proceed.'
granted (that) (admitting a counter-point): 'Granted that costs are high, the long-term benefits are clear.'
admittedly (admitting a partial truth): 'Admittedly, the plan has flaws, but it is the best available option.'

rules.pattern: ON THE CONTRARY vs ON THE OTHER HAND

examples.singular: on the contrary (= NOT what was said is true; the opposite is true)
examples.plural: Many believe technology isolates people. On the contrary, it can foster deeper connections.

examples.singular: on the other hand (presenting a different perspective, not contradiction)
examples.plural: Technology brings convenience. On the other hand, it raises privacy concerns.

rules.note: ❌ Critical error: using 'on the contrary' to mean 'on the other hand'. They are NOT synonyms. 'On the contrary' = the opposite is true; 'on the other hand' = a different perspective.

sections.title: 3. Cause / Effect / Result — Beyond 'Because' and 'So'
sections.summary: These linkers make logical relationships explicit. Vary them across an essay.

items: since (formal, especially when reason is known)
in that (formal: in the sense that)
due to + noun (Often used in formal writing)
rules.note: ❌ Critical error: 'Due to it rained heavily…' / 'Owing to he was late…'. These prepositions take NOUN, not a clause. ✔ 'Due to heavy rain…' / 'Owing to his lateness…' or use the conjunction form 'Because it rained heavily…'.

examples.plural: Costs have risen. Therefore, prices must increase.
Demand has surged; consequently, supply chains are strained.

rules.pattern: PURPOSE — to express WHY something is done

examples.plural: She studies hard to get a scholarship.
Governments invest in education in order to develop a skilled workforce.
He left early so as to avoid traffic.
She speaks slowly so that everyone can understand.
Strict rules apply in order that fairness is maintained.

sections.tip: Open Task 2 introductions with 'Given that…' or 'In light of…': 'In light of growing concerns about climate change, governments must act.' This single phrase signals Band 7+ register.

sections.title: 4. Similarity & Comparison
sections.summary: How to point out similarities. Useful in Task 1 and Task 2 comparison essays.

examples.plural: Sales of electric cars rose sharply between 2015 and 2020. Similarly, hybrid car sales increased over the same period.
While 60% of men were employed full-time, the figure for women was only 35%.
Education reform requires patience. Likewise, healthcare improvements take decades to show results.
Just as physical exercise benefits the body, mental challenges sharpen the mind.

sections.title: 5. Giving Examples — Beyond 'For Example'
sections.summary: Repeating 'for example' three times in an essay sounds Band 6. Vary your example markers.

items: such as + noun (no comma, integrated): 'subjects such as physics and chemistry'
to illustrate (this) / to give an example

examples.plural: Languages such as French and Spanish are widely studied.
For example, France introduced a 35-hour week. / France, for example, introduced a 35-hour week.
Two countries — namely, Norway and Sweden — lead in renewable energy.
Climate change affects developing countries, particularly those in coastal regions.
To illustrate this point, consider Singapore's water management strategy.
Many countries have adopted this policy. A case in point is Finland.

rules.pattern: ❌ Common error: 'such as' vs 'for example' confusion

examples.singular: ❌ Such as France introduced a 35-hour week.
examples.plural: ✔ For example, France introduced a 35-hour week.

examples.singular: ❌ Many countries, for example France and Germany, are members.
examples.plural: ✔ Many countries, such as France and Germany, are members. (or: ...for example, France and Germany... — both work, but 'such as' is cleaner)

rules.note: 'Such as' integrates examples into the noun phrase. 'For example' starts a sentence or a parenthetical.

sections.title: 6. Summarising & Concluding — Beyond 'In Conclusion'
sections.summary: Most candidates use 'In conclusion' for every essay. Vary it. Conclusions need rhythm too.

items: In conclusion (most common — fine, but don't ALWAYS use it)

examples.plural: In conclusion, both sides have valid arguments, but the long-term benefits of [X] outweigh the costs.
Overall, although [X] has its drawbacks, the advantages are considerable.
All things considered, governments must take stronger action on [X].
Ultimately, the success of any such policy depends on consistent enforcement.

sections.title: 7. Reference Devices — Pointing Back Across Sentences
sections.summary: Cohesion isn't just linkers. The HIGHEST cohesion comes from references — this/that/such/the former — which point back to earlier ideas without repeating them.

rules.pattern: THIS / THAT — pointing to a previous idea

examples.plural: Climate change is accelerating. This is a major problem. (vague — 'this' has no clear antecedent)
Climate change is accelerating. This trend is a major problem. (this trend = the previous idea)
Costs have risen sharply. This development has surprised analysts.

rules.note: ALWAYS pair 'this/that' with a noun in academic writing. 'This issue', 'this concern', 'this trend', 'this phenomenon', 'this approach', 'this development'.

rules.pattern: Useful summarising nouns to pair with 'this/such'
items: such concerns / such measures / such conditions

examples.plural: Both online and traditional learning have advantages: the former offers flexibility, while the latter provides social interaction.
Two main views exist: free-market and state-led. The former emphasises efficiency; the latter prioritises equality.

rules.note: Highly academic. Use sparingly — once per essay max.

rules.pattern: PRONOUNS for cohesion (it / they / he / she / them)

examples.plural: The government introduced new regulations. They have been controversial.
Climate change is real. It threatens millions of lives, and tackling it requires global cooperation.

rules.note: Don't repeat the noun if a pronoun is clear. But ensure the antecedent is unambiguous.

sections.tip: After writing a paragraph, find every full-noun repetition (e.g., 'pollution… pollution… pollution'). Replace at least half with 'this issue / this problem / it / such pollution'.

sections.title: 8. Substitution & Ellipsis
sections.summary: Replace repeated words with substitute pronouns or omit them entirely. Subtle Band 7+ cohesion.

examples.plural: I have a red car and a blue one. (one = car)
These ideas are better than the old ones. (ones = ideas)

rules.pattern: DO SO / DO THAT / DO IT — substituting whole verb phrases

examples.plural: I plan to revise the report; I will do so this evening.
Governments must act decisively, and they must do so urgently.

examples.singular: so = the previous statement is true
examples.plural: Will it rain? — I think so.

examples.singular: not = the previous statement is not true
examples.plural: Will he agree? — I hope not.
He works hard, and she does too. (does = works hard, omitted)
I wanted to go, but I wasn't able to. (able to GO is omitted)
He plays football and (he plays) cricket.

sections.title: 9. Paragraph-Level Cohesion
sections.summary: Cohesion isn't only inside sentences. Paragraphs link to each other with topic sentences, transitions, and parallel structure.

rules.pattern: TOPIC SENTENCE — first sentence of each body paragraph

examples.plural: [Linker] + [main claim of paragraph].
One major argument in favour of online learning is its flexibility.
A second consideration concerns the cost of implementation.
Critics, however, raise valid concerns about social isolation.

rules.note: Topic sentences signal the structure of your essay. Good topic sentences let an examiner skim the first sentence of each paragraph and grasp the entire argument.

examples.plural: [End of paragraph 1: ... cost-effectiveness.] [Start of paragraph 2: 'Beyond cost, there are also concerns about quality...']
Such concerns lead naturally to the question of regulation.
Paragraph 1: 'One advantage is X.' Paragraph 2: 'A further advantage lies in Y.' Paragraph 3: 'However, the most significant benefit is Z.'
Paragraph 1 (view 1): 'Some argue that…' Paragraph 2 (view 2): 'Others contend that…' Paragraph 3 (opinion): 'In my view, while both sides have merit…'

sections.title: 10. Top Cohesion Errors at the Band 6 → 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: Specific cohesion mistakes that signal Band 6 writing.

examples.plural: ✔ One key reason… A further consideration… Equally important… Ultimately…

examples.singular: ❌ Moreover, moreover, moreover (one per body paragraph)
examples.plural: ✔ Mix: moreover, in addition, what is more, furthermore, additionally.
✔ 'This trend is a problem.' / 'This phenomenon is a problem.'

examples.singular: ❌ Despite he was tired, he kept working.
examples.plural: ✔ Despite being tired… / Although he was tired…

examples.singular: ❌ Although it was raining, but we went out.
examples.plural: ✔ Although it was raining, we went out.

rules.pattern: 5. 'On the contrary' = 'on the other hand' confusion

examples.singular: ❌ Some prefer city life. On the contrary, others prefer the countryside.
examples.plural: ✔ Some prefer city life. On the other hand, others prefer the countryside. ('on the contrary' implies contradiction, not just difference)

examples.singular: ❌ Such as France introduced this policy.
examples.plural: ✔ For example, France introduced this policy.
✔ However, the policy is unpopular. (comma after 'However' at sentence start)
✔ The policy, however, is unpopular. (commas BOTH sides when mid-sentence)

rules.pattern: 8. Repeating the same noun across the paragraph

examples.plural: Pollution is bad. Pollution affects health. Pollution must be reduced. Pollution is increasing.
Pollution is bad. It affects health, particularly in urban areas. This problem must be addressed. Such challenges are only increasing.

examples.singular: ❌ Body paragraph starts with example or detail.
examples.plural: ✔ Body paragraph starts with a topic sentence stating the main claim, then examples support it.

rules.pattern: 10. Conclusion that just repeats the introduction

examples.singular: ❌ 'In conclusion, technology has changed our lives.' (= same as introduction)
examples.plural: ✔ 'In conclusion, while technology has undoubtedly transformed daily life, its long-term implications for human connection demand careful reflection.' (= synthesis, not repetition)

sections.tip: Read every essay aloud. Wherever you trip over a connector ('however' three times in a paragraph?), or where you sense the rhythm flatten ('Firstly… Secondly… Thirdly…'), that's the cohesion fix. Cohesion is FELT before it's seen.

checklist: I use at least 4 different addition linkers in a 250-word essay (NOT 'and / and / also / and').
I distinguish 'on the contrary' (the opposite is true) from 'on the other hand' (different perspective).
I never combine 'although … but' or 'because … so' in the same sentence.
After 'despite / in spite of', I use noun or -ing — never a clause.
After 'due to / owing to / because of', I use noun — never a clause.
I always pair 'this / that / such' with a summarising noun (this issue, such concerns).
I use 'the former / the latter' at least once per essay (when comparing two specific things).
I use 'such as' inline with the noun (subjects such as physics) but 'for example' to start a sentence.
I use varied conclusion openers (not always 'In conclusion').
I write a clear topic sentence at the start of every body paragraph.
I read essays aloud and reduce any noun repeated 3+ times to pronouns/references.
I use varied openers across body paragraphs (NOT 'Firstly / Secondly / Thirdly / Finally').
